help.maskIntro: A mask says which bits of the CAN ID must match. A 1 in the mask means "this bit must equal the ID bit"; a 0 means "don't care". An exact match needs every bit set (0x7FF for 11-bit IDs, 0x1FFFFFFF for 29-bit). Setting some bits to 0 broadens the match — useful for catching all IDs that share a prefix.
help.rangeIntro: Matches every CAN ID from Lo to Hi inclusive. Use Range when the IDs you want share no useful bit pattern, such as an arbitrary span like 0x100–0x1FF or 0x300–0x37F. Both bounds are inclusive; Hi must be ≥ Lo.
help.rangeExample: Example: Lo 0x100, Hi 0x1FF matches all 256 IDs from 0x100 through 0x1FF.

tooltips.bridgeId: Numeric identifier for this bridge rule on the device. Each rule needs a unique ID so it can be enabled, disabled, or removed individually. If you tick Bidirectional, the reverse rule uses the next ID up.
tooltips.sourceInterface: The interface frames are received from. Frames matching the filters below are forwarded to the destination interface.
tooltips.destInterface: The interface matched frames are transmitted on. The source and destination must be the same interface type (CAN to CAN, RS-485 to RS-485).
tooltips.bidirectional: Creates two bridge rules at once: source→destination, and destination→source. Use this when both ends of the bridge should mirror each other's traffic. The reverse rule reuses the same filter list.
tooltips.defaultAction: Sets what happens to frames that don't match any filter. Pass forwards everything by default and the filter list specifies IDs to drop (a deny list — useful for blocking a few noisy IDs). Block drops everything by default and the filter list specifies IDs to forward (an allow list — useful for forwarding only a few specific IDs). With no filters, Pass forwards every frame and Block drops every frame.
tooltips.filtersDeny: IDs listed here are dropped; everything else is forwarded. Add multiple entries to drop more IDs or ranges. RS-485 bridges ignore filters and always forward.
tooltips.filtersAllow: IDs listed here are forwarded; everything else is dropped. Add multiple entries to allow more IDs or ranges. RS-485 bridges ignore filters and always forward.
tooltips.kind: How this entry matches an incoming CAN ID. Mask matches a bit pattern — useful for catching all IDs that share the same prefix or suffix. Range matches every ID between the two values inclusive — useful for arbitrary low/high bounds like 0x100..0x1FF.
tooltips.ide: Restricts the entry to a specific frame format. Any matches both standard 11-bit and extended 29-bit frames. Std matches 11-bit only, Ext matches 29-bit only. Use Std or Ext when the same numeric ID can appear in both formats on the bus (e.g. mixed J1939 + legacy).
tooltips.aMask: The CAN ID to match. Bits set to 0 in the mask are ignored — set this to the value you want those bits to take when the mask bits are 1.
tooltips.aRange: Lower bound of the inclusive range. Frames with CAN IDs below this value are not matched.
tooltips.bMask: Bitmask. A 1 bit means 'this bit must match the CAN ID value'; a 0 bit means 'don't care'. Common values: 0x7FF for an exact 11-bit ID match, 0x1FFFFFFF for an exact 29-bit ID match, 0x700 to match the top 3 bits of an 11-bit ID.
tooltips.bRange: Upper bound of the inclusive range. Frames with CAN IDs above this value are not matched. Must be greater than or equal to the Lo value.
tooltips.helpToggle: Show or hide help for this filter — explains how this match type works, with an interactive calculator for masks.
